natural_sources: As a byproduct of copper, gold, lead, and zinc mining
natural_occurrence.description: Silver is a precious metal that occurs naturally in pure metallic form and in various ores. Food-grade silver is highly purified metallic silver, often in the form of very thin leaf or powder.

manufacturing.description: Produced by refining silver from ores or recycled sources, then hammered into extremely thin sheets (silver leaf) or ground into fine powder. Food-grade silver must be at least 99.9% pure.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used in luxury skincare products and cosmetics for antimicrobial properties and aesthetic appeal.
other_uses.industrial: Used in electronics, solar panels, mirrors, photography, and antimicrobial coatings.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used in wound dressings and antimicrobial medical devices (silver sulfadiazine).
other_uses.household: Silver jewelry, tableware, and decorative items.

eu.conditions: Only for external decoration of confectionery, chocolate products, and liqueurs. Decorative use only.

usa.classification: Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) for decorative purposes
usa.notes: Approved only for external decoration, not for general consumption

canada.notes: Permitted for surface decoration of confectionery

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (very limited absorption; use restricted to external decoration)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (negligible exposure due to decorative use only)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA confirms safety of silver for external decoration at current use levels

food_categories_eu.category_name: Decorations, coatings and fillings (external decoration only)

commonly_found_in: Luxury chocolates and pralines (decorative silver leaf)
Indian sweets - Barfi, Peda (varak silver leaf)
Gourmet desserts at fine dining restaurants
